papers.title: Effects of annealing temperature on microstructure and mechanical properties of a low density δ-TRIP steel plate via intercritical quenching-annealing process
papers.paper_scope: Investigated the effects of annealing temperature on microstructure, mechanical properties, austenite stability, and deformation behavior of Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C low-density steel plates processed by intercritical quenching-annealing, focusing on TRIP/TWIP effects and laminated microstructure evolution.

alloys.alloys_notes: Actual composition measured for the experimental steel prepared by vacuum induction furnace. Density measured as 7.34 g/cm³ by Drainage Method using Archimedes’ Principle, which is 6.7% lower than that of conventional low-carbon low-alloyed steels.

processes.description: Hot-rolled state: ingot homogenized and forged, then reheated to 1100°C for 2 h, hot rolled from 80 mm to 12 mm in eight passes with initial rolling temperature ~1050°C and finishing ~950°C, followed by air cooling to room temperature.
Intercritical quenching at 1050°C: hot-rolled plate reheated to 1050°C in the ferrite-austenite dual-phase region for 1 h, followed by water quenching.
Quenching-annealing at 620°C: water-quenched plate annealed at 620°C for 1 h, followed by air cooling.
processes.processes_notes: Carbides precipitated in tempered martensite region at this annealing temperature.

processes.description: Quenching-annealing at 680°C: water-quenched plate annealed at 680°C for 1 h, followed by air cooling.
Quenching-annealing at 740°C: water-quenched plate annealed at 740°C for 1 h, followed by air cooling.
processes.processes_notes: This condition produced the highest product of strength and elongation and the maximum uniform elongation among the QA series.

processes.description: Quenching-annealing at 800°C: water-quenched plate annealed at 800°C for 1 h, followed by air cooling.
processes.processes_notes: Some austenite underwent martensitic transformation during air cooling after annealing, resulting in new martensite in the tempered martensite region.

processing_steps.processing_steps_notes: Ingot heated to 1100°C for 2 h and forged into a square forging ingot measuring 80 mm × 70 mm.
Reheated to 1100°C and held for 2 h; initial rolling temperature approximately 1050°C and finishing rolling temperature approximately 950°C.
Cooled to room temperature after hot rolling.
Reheated to 1050°C in the ferrite and austenite dual-phase region.
Water quenched after holding at 1050°C.
Hot-rolled plate reheated to 1050°C in the ferrite-austenite dual-phase region.
Water quenched after intercritical heating.
Annealed at 620°C following water quenching.
Cooled to room temperature after annealing.
Hot-rolled plate reheated to 1050°C in the ferrite-austenite dual-phase region.
Water quenched after intercritical heating.
Annealed at 680°C following water quenching.
Cooled to room temperature after annealing.
Hot-rolled plate reheated to 1050°C in the ferrite-austenite dual-phase region.
Water quenched after intercritical heating.
Annealed at 740°C following water quenching.
Cooled to room temperature after annealing.
Hot-rolled plate reheated to 1050°C in the ferrite-austenite dual-phase region.
Water quenched after intercritical heating.
Annealed at 800°C following water quenching.
Cooled to room temperature after annealing; some austenite transformed to martensite during this air cooling.

structures.overall_structure: Laminated microstructure consisting of δ-ferrite bands and lath martensite, with retained austenite in martensite bands
Laminated microstructure inherited from hot-rolled state; δ-ferrite bands became thinner and discontinuous; prior austenite grain grew up
Laminated microstructure consisting of δ-ferrite bands and tempered martensite lamellar with fine reverted austenite grains and precipitated carbides

phases_present.morphology: fine grains among tempered martensitic laths

structures.overall_structure: Laminated microstructure consisting of δ-ferrite bands and tempered martensite lamellar with fine reverted austenite grains

structures.overall_structure: Laminated microstructure consisting of δ-ferrite bands and tempered martensite lamellar with fine reverted austenite grains and new martensite formed during air cooling

formed during air cooling after annealing

characterization_methods.technique: Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) with electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD)
characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructure observation, phase identification, austenite distribution mapping, grain boundary characterization, and GND density analysis
characterization_methods.key_findings: Laminated microstructure of δ-ferrite bands and tempered martensite lamellar; band contrast maps; austenite volume fractions by EBSD; grain boundary distributions (AG boundaries, high-angle packet boundaries, low-angle packet boundaries, block boundaries, sub-block boundaries); austenite grain size statistics.

characterization_methods.purpose: Measure austenite volume fraction before and after tensile tests, and estimate stacking fault energy by Rietveld refinement
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite volume fractions by XRD for QA specimens; SFP and microstrain parameters for SFE calculation; XRD profiles before and after tensile deformation.

characterization_methods.technique: Tensile testing using electronic tensile testing machine (WDW-100E)
characterization_methods.purpose: Measure mechanical properties at room temperature
characterization_methods.key_findings: Engineering stress-strain curves; yield strength, tensile strength, total elongation, yield-tensile ratio, and PSE obtained at strain rate of 0.375 min^-1 with rod-shaped sample size M10 × 60 mm and gauge length 25 mm, tensile axis parallel to rolling direction.

characterization_methods.purpose: Calculate equilibrium phase fraction diagram and composition partitioning as a function of temperature
characterization_methods.key_findings: Equilibrium phase fractions of ferrite, austenite, and cementite; A1 temperature ~617°C; elemental partitioning of C, Al, Mn between ferrite and austenite; quenching temperature 1050°C selected based on calculated results.

computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Quenching temperature 1050°C and annealing temperatures (620°C, 680°C, 740°C, 800°C) were selected based on calculated A1 temperature (~617°C) and austenite fraction evolution.

analytical_model.model_name: GND density from EBSD KAM maps
analytical_model.parameters: Burger's vector b: 2.887 Å for BCC, 2.563 Å for FCC; step size μ: 150 nm
analytical_model.model_note: Equations: ρGND = 2 KAMave / (μb); KAMave = exp((1/N) Σ ln KAMi)

analytical_model.equation_role: Estimate austenite SFE from XRD Rietveld refinement parameters
analytical_model.parameters: Zener elastic anisotropy A, shear modulus G<111>, lattice constant a0, root mean square microstrain ε50, stacking fault probability SFP

analytical_model.equation_role: Quantify austenite stability during tensile deformation
analytical_model.model_note: Equation: Vε = Vo exp(-kε), where higher k means lower austenitic stability

computational_details.simulation_method: Python machine learning model for crystallographic boundary identification
computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Used to identify austenitic grain boundaries, high-angle packet boundaries, low-angle packet boundaries, block boundaries, and sub-block boundaries from EBSD data, and calculate corresponding boundary densities.

unmapped_findings: Work hardening rate (WHR) curves of QA specimens exhibited three stages based on slope: stage I (ε < 0.01) with sharp decrease related to soft phase deformation and dislocation movement; stage II (0.01 < ε < 0.15) with slight decrease attributed to competition between dislocation gliding and hardening mechanisms including TRIP effect; stage III (ε > 0.15) with slowly increasing WHR dominated by TRIP effect and possible TWIP effect.

Stacking fault energies (SFE) of austenite estimated by XRD Rietveld refinement: QA-620 ~36.50 mJ/m², QA-680 ~38.27 mJ/m², QA-740 ~33.29 mJ/m², QA-800 ~31.53 mJ/m², indicating TWIP effect should exist during deformation.

Austenite mechanical stability constants (k) calculated from austenite volume fraction before and after tensile tests: QA-620 ~1.24, QA-680 ~0.56, QA-740 ~1.47, QA-800 ~1.50. Higher k value indicates lower austenitic stability.
Products of tensile strength and total elongation (PSE): HR ~6.2 GPa%, IQ-1050 ~1.9 GPa%, QA-620 ~38.0 GPa%, QA-680 ~33.5 GPa%, QA-740 ~45.2 GPa%, QA-800 ~36.2 GPa%.

QA-680 exhibited necking at around 25% strain due to deficient TRIP effect, but afterward underwent a second stage of uniform elongation until failure because metastable austenite produced TRIP effect after necking.

Grain boundary densities (relative units from EBSD): for QA specimens, austenitic grain (AG) boundary density increased with annealing temperature until 800°C then decreased, while block boundary (>45°) density decreased continuously with annealing temperature.

Fracture morphology of QA specimens: mixed fracture features with cleavage in δ-ferrite (river pattern, cleavage step), quasi-cleavage, and dimples; laminar tears observed due to laminated microstructure; second cracks initiated at interfaces between δ-ferrite and γ/α phases.
Tensile testing conditions: strain rate of 0.375 min^-1, rod-shaped sample size M10 × 60 mm with gauge length 25 mm, tensile axis parallel to rolling direction.
For QA-800, new martensite formed from austenite during air cooling after annealing at 800°C, as evidenced in SEM micrographs.
The uniform elongation of all QA specimens except QA-680 was almost equal to total elongation; QA-740 had maximum uniform elongation of ~62.4%.
Austenite grain size statistics from EBSD before tensile tests showed that around 90% of austenite grains in QA specimens were smaller than 4.8 μm.
